decision_summary: Constraints are satisfied with a significant margin (58.6°C vs 85.0°C limit). However, optimization progress has stalled. To break the plateau and further minimize chip temperature, I will execute a coordinated multi-variable change: increasing the heat spreader's physical dimensions (width and height) to enhance lateral spreading and thermal cross-section, while simultaneously increasing its conductivity. This leverages the 'JointChanges' strategy recommended for when single-variable tuning yields diminishing returns.

changes.reason: Maximize spreader width to the domain boundary (1.0m) to capture more lateral heat flow from the chip edges.
Increase spreader thickness by 20% to reduce vertical thermal resistance and increase heat capacity.
Boost conductivity by 20% to complement the geometric expansion, ensuring the larger volume effectively transports heat.

expected_effects: More uniform temperature distribution across the base plate due to wider spreading
Breaking of the optimization plateau observed in recent iterations
risk_notes: Spreader width is now at the maximum editable bound (1.0m); future geometry improvements must focus on height or position.
Conductivity increase pushes material properties higher; ensure physical realism if this represents a specific alloy.
Coordinated changes make it harder to isolate the sensitivity of individual variables in post-analysis.
